coredata.dc:title: Preparation of biomass-waste-derived carbon dots from apricot shell for highly sensitive and selective detection of ascorbic acid
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, CAS. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, CAS. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: XU, Hong-Bo
ZHOU, Sheng-Hai
LI, Ming-Yue
ZHANG, Pei-Ran
WANG, Zi-Han
TIAN, Yan-Mei
WANG, Xu-Qin
coredata.dc:description: An ascorbic acid (AA) fluorescence sensor was built using biomass-waste-derived carbon dots (CDs). The CDs were prepared using apricot shell biomass, which serves as an abundant starting source. The prepared CDs displayed good dispersion, uniform particle size, high photostability, and a greenish-yellow luminescence. The experimental results confirmed the greenish-yellow fluorescence was selectively quenched by Fe3+ and then recovered selectively upon adding AA to the Fe3+-CD system, exhibiting an off-on phenomenon. The recovered fluorescence intensity was linear with AA concentration. The linear range is 1–100 μM and detection limit is 40 nM. Analysis of a real sample was also carried out to determine the AA content in human urine. The proposed strategy allowed efficient use of discarded apricot shells that have good economic, environmental, and medical benefits.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Apricot shells
Carbon dot
Fluorescence sensor
Ascorbic acid

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) XRD patterns of apricot-shell-derived carbon (curve a) and apricot-shell-derived carbon without removal of Zn (curve b). (B) N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of apricot-shell-derived carbon. Inset: enlarged N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms for relative pressures ranging from 0.34 to 1.0.
(A) Typical TEM image, (B) XPS full spectrum, (C) C 1 s and (D) N 1 s spectra of the CDs.

(A) UV-vis absorption spectra of Fe3+ (black curve), CDs (green curve) and CDs+Fe3+ (red curve). Inset is a comparison of quantum dots irradiated by sunlight (a) and 365 nm ultraviolet light (b). (B) Fluorescent spectra of CDs with and without Fe3+. (C) Influence of several metal ions including Co2+, Ni2+, Hg2+, Na+, Mn2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Fe3+, Ag+, Cu2+, Bi3+, K+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Zn2+ and Al3+ on the fluorescence of CDs. F and F 0 designate the fluorescence intensity with and without metal ion, respectively.

(A) The fluorescent intensity of CDs at varied concentrations (from left to right: 17.2, 34.4, 51.6, 68.8, 86 mg/L). (B) Plot of Fe3+-CDs systems fluorescence versus incubation time. F and F 0 designate the fluorescence intensity with and without AA, respectively.

(A) Fluorescent response of the CD solution with 50 μM Fe3+towards AA (from bottom to top: 1, 3, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80, 90 and 100 μM). (B) F/F 0 versus AA concentration. F and F 0 designate the fluorescence intensity with and without AA, respectively.

(A) Selectivity of CDs-based sensor for ascorbic acid and several physiological molecules (Cys, Fru, Et, Glu, Gly, UA, OA, DA, lle, Arg, Ser, Ala, and GSH). F 0 and F designate the fluorescent intensity of the mixture solution (50 μM Fe3+and CDs) without and with biomolecules, respectively. (B) Photostability of CDs irradiated by xenon lamp at different times.
Recovery for AA in a human urine sample (n = 3).
Performance comparison of the developed sensor with other AA sensor.

row.entry: ZIF-8/platinum nanoparticles modified glassy carbon electrode
Mercaptopropionic acid capped CdTe quantum dots
GQDs functionalized with nitroxide free radicals
S-, N-doped GQDs functionalized with nitroxide free radicals

head.ce:title: Preparation of biomass-waste-derived carbon dots from apricot shell for highly sensitive and selective detection of ascorbic acid

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hebei Normal University for Nationalities, Chengde 067000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hebei Normal University for Nationalities, Chengde 067000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An ascorbic acid (AA) fluorescence sensor was built using biomass-waste-derived carbon dots (CDs). The CDs were prepared using apricot shell biomass, which serves as an abundant starting source. The prepared CDs displayed good dispersion, uniform particle size, high photostability, and a greenish-yellow luminescence. The experimental results confirmed the greenish-yellow fluorescence was selectively quenched by Fe3+ and then recovered selectively upon adding AA to the Fe3+-CD system, exhibiting an off-on phenomenon. The recovered fluorescence intensity was linear with AA concentration. The linear range is 1–100 μM and detection limit is 40 nM. Analysis of a real sample was also carried out to determine the AA content in human urine. The proposed strategy allowed efficient use of discarded apricot shells that have good economic, environmental, and medical benefits.

ce:section.ce:para: Traditional quantum dots usually refer to metal-based semiconductor quantum dots (MSCQDs), which are inorganic nanocrystals composed of a metal-based semiconductor material with 10–105 atoms [1]. MSCQDs exhibit adjustable fluorescence, good chemical stability, and high photostability; thus, they have considerable value in applications such as laser, sensing, solar energy conversion, and photocatalysis [2]. However, their applicability in the above-mentioned fields is hindered by their heavy-metal toxicity and weak dispersity [3]. With the ongoing in-depth research on fluorescence quantum dots, carbon dots (CDs) are replacing traditional MSCQDs because of their low toxicity, good water solubility, and abundance of nontoxic raw materials [4]. Several efforts have been devoted toward the preparation of CDs by utilizing various carbon-rich precursors such as biomass carbon sources (e.g., bamboo leaves, potato, and garlic), large inorganic carbon structures (e.g., soot, carbon nanotubes and graphite), and small molecules including phenylenediamine and citric acid [5–7]. Among them, biomass carbon sources, especially biomass waste, are widely utilized as starting materials for the preparation of CDs because (a) biomass waste is rich in resources and is environmentally benign [8]; and (b) biomass waste can be used for the preparation of value-added CDs, which can reduce preparation costs, prevent environmental pollution, and provide new pathways for waste management [9]. Thus, research on new types of biomass waste for the preparation of quantum dots is vital.

Usually, biomass-derived CDs are synthesized through a bottom-up approach [8]. For the bottom-up synthesis of CDs, biomass carbon sources, such as agarose waste [10] and honey [11], are grown into nano-sized CDs, usually by hydrothermal treatment [12]. The advantages of hydrothermal treatment include ease of operation, low cost, and mild experimental conditions. However, this method has considerable drawbacks such as the incomplete carbonization of biomass, and difficulty in the separation of the byproduct molecular fluorophores from the quantum dots [13]. Recently, a new up-down joint approach has been developed through a combination of both the bottom-up and top-down techniques, which enables large-scale synthesis of well-dispersed CDs from biomass waste [14]. For example, Wang et al. used the joint route for the synthesis of CDs, wherefor widely available rice husk biomass was used as the source [15]. First, rice husk was converted to bulk carbon flakes by a "bottom-up" method. Following this, the prepared rice husk carbon was sonicated in an acidic environment, and CDs were subsequently obtained by hydrothermal cutting in an alkaline environment through a "top-down" cutting method. This up-down joint approach establishes the feasibility of preparing high-value CDs from solid waste [13]. However, research on the up-down joint preparation of CDs using apricot shell waste as a raw precursor is scarce.

Ascorbic acid (AA) is a water-soluble vitamin. It plays a key role in maintaining various functions of human metabolism [16]. AA deficiency can cause scurvy, arteriosclerosis, and anemia, while excessive AA can cause uric acid stones and bone diseases [17]. AA can easily combine with O, making it an inhibitor of enzymatic browning of vegetables and fruits. It is also used as an antioxidant in beer, non-alcoholic beverages, and fruit juices [18]. Therefore, accurate detection of AA is of importance in both clinical diagnosis as well as the food industry. To date, researchers have developed many methods to detect ascorbic acid, including electrochemical assays [17,18], colorimetry [19,20], fluorescence methods [21], and chromatographic techniques [22]. Among these, fluorescence methods have attracted particular research interest owing to their ease of operation, good reproducibility, and high sensitivity [23]. In fluorescence assays, various fluorescence probes, including MSCQDs, metal nanoparticles, and most recently applied CDs, have been employed for sensitive and effective determination of AA [24–26]. Compared to MSCQDs and metal nanoparticles, CDs have the inherent advantages of low toxicity and good water solubility [27]. In contrast, due to differences in preparation routes and conditions, CDs show huge variations in sizes, heteroatom dopants, functional groups, and other parameters, thus exhibiting different sensing properties [28]. Therefore, the development of novel CDs for AA detection is necessary.

Herein, considering the low environmental impact, abundance of resources, low cost, reasonable disposal of biomass waste, and applicability to high-performance AA sensors, the development of new CDs, with waste biomass as a precursor, for effective detection of AA was undertaken. Waste apricot shell was converted to CDs by carbonization in an inert atmosphere, followed by top-down acid-oxidation cleavage. Then, the structural information and optical properties of the as-prepared CDs were determined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), ultraviolet visible and fluorescent spectroscopy, etc. Finally, the prepared Fe3+/CDs mixture was utilized to detect selectively AA.

Herein, all chemicals involved in the work were used directly after purchase. And ultrapure water was utilized to prepare the solutions. X-ray diffraction (XRD) (SmartLab3000W, Rigaku, Japan), N2 adsorption apparatus (Quantachrome Instruments, United States), XPS (Thermo Fisher Scientific, United States), atomic force microscopy (AFM) (Bruker, United States), transmission electron microscope (TEM) (JEOL, JEM-3010, Japan), and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) (Nicolet Magna 5 PC, United States) were used to obtain the microstructure and morphology of carbon-based nanomaterials. The UV-vis spectra of the CDs were by a UV-vis spectrophotometer (Aoyi Instrument Shanghai Co., Ltd., A590, China). Their fluorescence spectra were obtained by a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, F-7000, Japan).

As shown in Fig. 1 , the waste apricot shell was first crushed and soaked in ZnCl2 solution (solid-to-liquid ratio of 1:2) for 24 h. After drying in an oven at 80 °C, the above mixture turned black and was transferred to a tubular furnace and subsequently carbonized at 850 °C in a N2 atmosphere. Finally, apricot-shell-derived carbon was obtained after the removal of Zn residue with HCl.

Following a previously reported method [29], 1.5 mL concentrated nitric acid was poured into an autoclave. After this, a glass steamer containing 0.2 g apricot-shell-derived carbon was placed in the above autoclave. After heating the autoclave for 3 h, the CDs were formed and subsequently collected using ultrapure water via in-situ filtration.

First, the CDs and Fe3+were stirred with ultrapure water to obtain 2 mL of the Fe3+-CDs detection system, with a pH of approximately 2.57, CD concentration of 68.8 mg/L, and Fe3+ concentration of 50 μM. After this, varying amounts of AA were added into the Fe3+-CDs system and incubated for 3 min. Finally, the fluorescent spectra of the resulting solutions were measured in the range of 350–650 nm, in which excitation wavelength, emission slit and excitation slit was 340, 5, 10 nm, respectively.

For the detection of AA in human urine, 500 μL of the fresh sample was mixed by a solution (1.5 mL) comprising of 4.0 mM Na2EDTA and 2.0 M HClO4. After 30 min, the above mixture needs to be centrifuged for 20 min at 10,000 r/min. After that, take out the centrifuge tube was taken out and the supernatant was collected. Then the supernatant was filtered with a microfiltration membrane (0.22 μM) and transferred to refrigerator for subsequent fluorescence analysis. In the fluorescence test, the stored urine samples were firstly mixed with water in a volume ratio of 1:99. Then, a 100 μL aliquot of the pretreated urine samples was added to 1900 μL of the Fe3+-CD system, followed by the addition of a calculated amount of AA. After incubation, the fluorescence intensity was measured using the proposed method and the results were analyzed.

In this work, fluorescence quantum yield was determined by a reference method, in which quinine sulfate (QS) was used as the standard (54%). Firstly, the absorbance of quinine sulfate and CD aqueous solution was measured by UV-vis spectrophotometer. Secondly, fluorescence intensity of the corresponding quinine sulfate and CD aqueous solution was obtained by fluorescence spectrophotometer. Thirdly, the quantum yield was calculated based on the equation:
(1)
Q
CDs
=
Q
QS
(
η
CDs
2
/
η
QS
2
)
(
A
QS
/
A
CDs
)
(
I
CDs
/
I
QS
)
where Q, ƞ, I and A are the quantum yield, refractive index of the water, fluorescence intensity and absorbance at exited wavelength, respectively.

Fig. 2 A (curve a) exhibits the wide angle XRD pattern of the apricot-shell-derived carbon material. The XRD pattern showed two broad diffraction peaks located at 24.8° and 43.2°, similar to a previous report [30]. The width and location of the peaks suggest that an almost amorphous or nanocrystalline carbon was formed [30]. In addition, unlike the XRD pattern of the carbon material before the removal of Zn residues using HCl (curve b in Fig. 2A), no diffraction peaks of Zn impurities were observed in the sample after HCl treatment. This result demonstrates that Zn was completely removed. SEM/EDS was used to further investigate the elemental makeup of the carbon material. The C and O contents in the material were 97.72% and 2.28%, respectively. Zn was not detected in the carbon material, which agrees well with the results of wide-angle XRD. Further, the porous structure of the synthesized carbon was analyzed by N2 adsorption technology, because ZnCl2 is a good activating agent for the preparation of nanoporous carbon. Fig. 2B presents a N2 adsorption isotherm of the prepared sample at −196.15 °C. The carbon material exhibited a combined type I and IV isotherm with an unobvious hysteresis loop (inset of Fig. 2B), which is in agreement with a previous report [31]. The adsorption of N2 occurred at a low relative pressure, and the adsorption capacity was high, indicating that the sample contains a microporous structure. At higher relative pressures, an unobvious hysteresis loop, caused by the capillary condensation effect, indicates the presence of mesopores. Therefore, the prepared carbon material has a hierarchical porous structure. Furthermore, the specific surface area, micropore size, and mesopore diameter of the apricot-shell-derived carbon were approximately 1487 m2/g, 0.4, and 3.4 nm, respectively, according to its N2 adsorption data. The large BET surface area and hierarchical porous structure of the carbon sample provided a large reaction interface and hence aided the rapid diffusion/transportation of HNO3 vapor scissors for the preparation of CDs.

TEM was utilized to investigate the structure of CDs synthesized from apricot shell biomass. As shown in Fig. 3 A, the CDs were well dispersed, showing the innate advantage of the top-down cutting strategy. Furthermore, particle size is in the range of 1.77–6.09 nm, which is relatively narrow (inset of Fig. 3A). The average diameter was approximately 3.8 nm. XPS was also employed to obtain the compositional and structural information of the CDs. In the survey XPS spectrum (Fig. 3B), the CDs exhibited a weak band for N 1s at 401 eV and strong peaks for O 1s and C 1s at 532 and 286 eV, respectively, indicating that the CDs were composed of N, C, and O elements. As discussed previously, SEM/EDS characterization confirmed that apricot shell carbon is composed of C and O, but not N. Therefore, HNO3 vapor, which was supplied as the cutting scissor, was likely the source for the observed nitrogen content. The corresponding C, O, and N contents were 57.5%, 36.5%, and 6%, respectively. Fig. 3C shows the high-resolution spectrum of C 1s, which could be divided into four peaks: 288.7, 285.5, 284.9, and 284.3 eV for CO, CO/CN, CC, and sp2 hybridized C groups, respectively [32,33]. Fig. 3D exhibits a high-resolution spectrum of O 1s, which has two deconvoluted peaks at 532.7 eV for COH and 531.6 eV for CO [34]. The high O content and O groups of CDs could be beneficial for sensing applications in aqueous solutions, as discussed in the subsequent section. The height of the prepared CDs also was measured using the atomic force microscope and exhibited in Fig. S1A. The height of CDs is less than 4 nm (Fig. S1B).

Fig. 4 A depicts the UV-vis absorption spectrum of CDs, which exhibits a broad absorption below 610 nm. Meanwhile, a weak absorption band can be found at about 227 nm, which is ascribed to a C=C bond related π→π* transition, as pointed out previously [35]. The inset of Fig. 4A shows the greenish-yellow luminescence emitted by the CD solution under UV light (365 nm). And, fluorescence spectra of CDs before and after adding iron ions were compared to study the fluorescence properties of CDs. The fluorescent intensity of CDs obviously decreased when iron ions were added (Fig. 4B). Moreover, the fluorescence response of CDs toward other metal ions was also investigated under the same test conditions, including Co2+, Ni2+, Hg2+, Na+, Mn2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Ag+, Cu2+, Bi3+, K+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, and Al3+ (Fig. 4C). The fluorescent intensity of CDs did not noticeably decrease after adding these ions into the CD solution. As suggested in some previous studies [36–38], the efficient coordination of iron ions and phenolic hydroxyl groups is the main reason for the high selectivity. The surface of the prepared CDs is rich in O-containing groups, which was confirmed by XPS characterization. To further investigate the reasons for the high selectivity of CDs to Fe3+, the UV-vis spectra of Fe3+ and CDs containing Fe3+ were measured and displayed in Fig. 4A. The absorption bands of Fe3+and CDs disappeared when Fe3+ was added to the CD solution, demonstrating that aggregation of CDs occurred in the presence of iron ions [39]. The Fe3+-CDs aggregates may result in the observed fluorescence quenching [37], suggesting the fluorescence sensing potential of the as-prepared CDs.

Recently, some novel fluorescent sensors have previously been fabricated to detect AA using an "off-on" strategy [40,41]. Herein, considering that Fe3+can be reduced to Fe2+by AA and hence destroy the specific coordination of Fe3+ and CDs, an off-on fluorescent sensor can be constructed for the detection of AA.

The fluorescence of CDs at different concentrations was measured to study the influence of concentration on the fluorescence performance. The fluorescence intensity of CDs increases first and decrease afterwards with an increase in CD concentration in the range of 17.2 to 86 mg/L (Fig. 5 A). When the CD concentration is 68.8 mg/L, the intensity reaches a maximum. This phenomenon may be caused by an increase in collision probability with an increase in the number of CDs, resulting in an aggregation of CD particles and a subsequent decrease in fluorescence intensity. Thus, a CD concentration of 68.8 mg/L was chosen as optimal for the fluorescent detection of AA. The influence of incubation time on the recovered fluorescence of the Fe3+-CDs system with AA was also investigated and exhibited in Fig. 5B. The fluorescent intensity of the Fe3+-CDs system proportionally increased with the incubation time from 1 to 3 min. The curve was observed to increase only slightly for incubation times longer than 3 min. Therefore, 3 min of incubation time was chosen for the fluorescent detection of AA.

The fluorescence response induced by varied amount of AA was evaluated to determine the functionality of the constructed sensing system for AA detection. The fluorescent spectrum of the Fe3+-CDs mixed solution exhibits fluorescence recovery on addition of AA (Fig. 6 A). The recovery intensity increases linearly with AA concentration ranging from 1 μM to 100 μM (Fig. 6B). The linear regression equation obtained is: F/F 0 = 0.9865 + 0.02935 × C AA (R = 0.997). The detection limit was about 0.04 μM based on the 3σ/S value, where S and σ represent the slope of the fitting line and the standard deviation of the blank, respectively.

To assess the selectivity of the AA sensor, the influences of some physiological molecules, such as Cysteine (Cys), fructose (Fru), ethanol (Et), glucose (Glu), glycine (Gly), uric acid (UA), oleic acid (OA), dopamine (DA), isoleucine (lle), arginine (Arg), serine (Ser), alanine (Ala), and glutathione (GSH), on the sensor were evaluated at a concentration of 80 μM. As shown in Fig. 7 A, these physiological molecules caused only a slight interference, indicating good selectivity. Therefore, the constructed sensor shows exceptional applicability to AA detection in real samples. The photostability of quantum dots is also an important indicator for evaluation of the sensor, as photostability reflects whether the fluorescence signal is stable and hence reflects the accuracy of the detection results. For the photostability study, a Xe lamp with 150 W was utilized to irradiate the prepared CDs for 2 h. The results (Fig. 7B) demonstrated that the fluorescence intensity of CDs increased by only 10%, suggesting relatively good photostability. Considering that the fluorescence of CDs is affected by its surface state [42], the surface state of CDs may change under the continuous irradiation of a Xe lamp, resulting in an increase in fluorescence intensity [43]. Furthermore, the quantum yield of the CDs was about 3.84%, which is comparable to, or higher than, that of some previously reported biomass-based CDs, as shown in Table S1.

Further, the proposed fluorescent sensor was used to detect AA in human urine samples. The results are shown in Table 1 . The recovery was 92.71%–96.15%, indicating the practical application and good reliability of the constructed sensor for ascorbic acid detection. Additionally, the results of our assay are close to the results of electrochemical pulse voltammetry (9.5, 29.1, 41.2 μM, respectively) for the same sample, suggesting good accuracy.

Moreover, a comparison of AA detection using the CDs sensor, developed in this study, with that of other relevant sensors is listed in Table 2 . The performance of the proposed sensor was better than, or comparable to, that of the other sensors, based on its lower detection limit and wider detection range. CDs prepared from discarded apricot shells thus show promising results in sensitive and low-cost detection of ascorbic acid in aqueous solutions.

In summary, discarded apricot shells were utilized as starting materials for the low-cost synthesis of CDs via an up-down joint method. A novel fluorescence sensor was constructed to rapidly detect ascorbic acid based on the "off-on" phenomenon of CDs in the presence of Fe3+. The proposed fluorescent sensor exhibited high selectivity toward AA, a low detection limit of 40 nM, a wide detection range in 1–100 μM, and practical applicability for the determination of AA in human urine. This work provided a highly efficient, economical, and environment-friendly CD-based fluorescence method for sensitive detection of AA.

Hong-Bo XU: Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Sheng-Hai ZHOU: Supervision. Ming-Yue LI: Methodology. Pei-Ran ZHANG: Visualization. Zi-Han WANG: Visualization. Yan-Mei TIAN: Formal analysis. Xu-Qin WANG: Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Hebei Province Natural and Scientific Foundation (B2017101022 and B2017101018). Additionally, the authors greatly appreciate the financial support from Hebei Province Educational Bureau (QN2017408 and BJ2018208).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.cjac.2022.100168.
